voiceover.text: Here's your SQL 600 Healthcare readout for April. Forty-three accounts, one story: momentum is real, but it's concentrated.

data.headline:
#4 of 13 SQL600 industries.
$15.4M ACR · +23% YoY.
$196.3M annualized growth since June '25.
data.body: After oscillating in a $13.5M–$14.4M band for 8 months, HLS broke out to $15.4M — a net +$1.76M (+13%) FY26 gain. HLS ACR now sits 76% above the SQL600 industry average of $8.7M, with the highest committed pipeline of any non-Software industry.
data.footnote: Source: SQL 600 Performance Tracking + Azure All-in-One · Generated 2026-04-20

scenes.notes: fade-lines preserves inline HTML — each line cascades in with <em>/<strong> rendering as gradient-filled numbers.

voiceover.text: HLS ranks fourth out of thirteen SQL 600 industries — and after eight months of oscillating between thirteen-and-a-half and fourteen-and-a-half million, we finally broke out. That's a net plus-thirteen percent FY26 gain, and HLS ACR is now seventy-six percent above the SQL600 industry average.

data.headline:
31 of 43 accounts carry
zero committed pipe.
data.body: UnitedHealth alone holds 68% of committed pipe. Meanwhile, Pharma's $66.7M uncommitted sits entirely untouched — Pfizer alone drives $69M in pipeline with a 101:1 uncommitted-to-committed ratio. Provider's 25 accounts hold $40.8M uncommitted but only $168K committed.

scenes.notes: fade-lines cascades each line in; gradient-filled <strong> numbers carry the emphasis without a confusing highlight bar.

voiceover.text: But here's the catch. UnitedHealth holds sixty-eight percent of committed pipe. The Pharma vertical has a hundred-to-one uncommitted-to-committed ratio — Pfizer alone has sixty-nine million in pipeline that's entirely uncommitted. And Provider's twenty-five accounts hold forty million in uncommitted pipe but only a hundred and sixty-eight thousand committed.
Let's start with the full portfolio snapshot.

scenes.notes: Open-the-book scorecard. Emphasize ACR and Committed Pipe — that's the new-high story.

voiceover.text: Two numbers to anchor on: fifteen-point-four million in ACR — a new high — and twelve-point-eight million in committed pipe, which finally moved after months of stalling. The uncommitted side sits at a hundred and sixty-three million, so there's plenty of upside if we can convert.

data.heading: Momentum
data.subheading: March broke the $15M ceiling for the first time in FY26

voiceover.text: Now let's look at how we got here.

data.title: ACR Trajectory — FY26
data.annotation: March rebound: +10.9% MoM, +23% YoY. After 8 months in a $13.5M–$14.4M band, the breakout to $15.4M = net +$1.76M (+13%) FY26 gain. Triggered by first major committed-pipe conversion (UHG + Elevance).

voiceover.text: Watch the chart: eight months of oscillating between thirteen-and-a-half and fourteen-and-a-half million, then a clean breakout in March. That one-point-seven-six million net gain from July start lined up exactly with UnitedHealth and Elevance converting committed pipeline.
March by the numbers — ten-point-nine percent month over month, twenty-three percent year over year, and the first meaningful committed-pipe conversion of the fiscal year.

data.heading: Concentration Risk
data.subheading: Where the ACR and the pipeline actually live

voiceover.text: Good growth numbers, but let's be honest about where that growth is actually coming from.

data.title: HLS ACR by Vertical — $15.4M across 43 accounts
data.annotation: Payor (6 accts): $8.2M ACR, $11.2M committed, $49.3M uncommitted. Provider (25 accts): $1.8M ACR, $168K committed, $40.8M uncommitted. Pharma: $660K committed vs $66.7M uncommitted — 101:1 ratio.

voiceover.text: Six Payor accounts generate over half the ACR. But look at Provider — twenty-five accounts, forty million in uncommitted pipe, and only a hundred and sixty-eight thousand committed. And Pharma's even more stark: a hundred-to-one uncommitted-to-committed ratio. Both segments are essentially dormant.

data.title: Committed Pipe Concentration — Top 5 Accounts
data.annotation: UHG = 68% of HLS committed pipe. Diversification into HCA ($32.2M uncommitted) and Elevance ($12.5M uncommitted) is the highest-leverage move.

voiceover.text: UnitedHealth is sixty-eight percent of committed pipe on its own. Getting HCA and Elevance to convert even a fraction of their uncommitted would dramatically change the diversification picture.

scenes.notes: Highlight UHG (dependency), Elevance (next conversion), HCA (largest latent opportunity).

voiceover.text: Here's the account-level view. Three names to watch: UnitedHealth for dependency risk, Elevance as the most likely next commit, and HCA sitting on thirty-two million uncommitted with nothing committed.

data.heading: Renewal Pressure
data.subheading: ~71.5K SQL cores up for renewal in FY26 across 24 accounts — only 1 has committed pipe

voiceover.text: Now the part that should keep us up at night — seventy-one thousand five hundred SQL cores across twenty-four accounts are up for renewal in Q3 and Q4. Only one of those twenty-four has committed pipeline.

data.title: SQL Cores at Renewal — FY26
data.annotation: Q4 renewal: ~62K cores (21 accounts), 18 with no committed pipe. Q3: ~9.5K cores (3 accounts), 2 with no committed pipe. Highest risk: Kaiser (13,872) and Advocate (8,670), no Arc, no commit.

voiceover.text: The red bars are the problem. Q4 alone has nearly fifty thousand cores with no Arc coverage across twenty-one accounts — eighteen of them with zero committed pipe. That's GCP's entire shopping list in our portfolio.

risks.context: 13,872 SQL cores · no Arc · $0 committed pipe. Largest single renewal exposure — GCP's #1 target in the portfolio.
8,670 cores · no Arc · no committed pipe. Large Q4 exposure with minimal realized cloud footprint.
2,702 cores · only 8% Arc attach · $102K committed. Needs acceleration before Q3 close.
Only 1 of 21 FY26-Q4 renewal accounts (IQVIA, $360K) carries any committed pipeline. BD (93% Arc) and IQVIA (84% Arc) are better positioned but still have zero committed modernization deals.

voiceover.text: Kaiser is the single biggest exposure — nearly fourteen thousand cores, no Arc, no committed pipe. Advocate adds eight thousand six hundred more in Q4. The broader pattern: only one of twenty-four renewal accounts has committed pipeline. Even BD and IQVIA, who have the strongest Arc adoption at ninety-three and eighty-four percent, have no committed modernization deals.

data.heading: GCP Leakage & Conversion Upside
data.subheading: 31 of 43 HLS accounts carry zero committed pipe

voiceover.text: Zooming out — this is the conversion opportunity and the GCP leakage risk, side by side.

right.label: Zero-Commit GCP Exposure (31 accounts)
items: HCA Healthcare — 21,100 cores · $32.2M uncommitted · zero committed
Kaiser Permanente — 13,872 cores · Q4 renewal · no Arc
CommonSpirit Health — 13,794 cores · no pipe of any kind
Epic Systems — 12,296 cores · zero pipeline of any kind
Advocate Health — 8,670 cores · Q4 renewal · no Arc · no commit

voiceover.text: On the left, twelve accounts carrying all the committed pipe. On the right, thirty-one accounts with zero commits. The top five gap accounts — HCA, Kaiser, CommonSpirit, Epic, and Advocate — hold sixty-nine thousand seven hundred and sixty SQL cores of unprotected exposure. That's a third of all HLS SQL cores.

stats.label: Factory Attach Rate
stats.description: 203 mod opps · only ~26 with Factory engineering

voiceover.text: The execution gap in three numbers: thirty-one accounts with no commits, a twelve-percent Factory attach rate, and twelve accounts with no modernization pipeline whatsoever.

data.heading: Decisions & Asks
data.subheading: What we need from leadership this quarter

voiceover.text: So — what do we need from leadership.

items.text: Lock Kaiser Permanente DBC motion before FY26-Q4 renewal — Arc pilot + exec sponsor alignment.
Convert HCA's $32.2M uncommitted (Capgemini partnership) into committed pipe this quarter.
Accelerate Elevance Dagabah SQL modernization ($10.8M) — highest-probability commit expansion.
Double Factory attach on the 203 mod opps from 12.8% to 25% — engineering depth over breadth.
Validate the $1.3B WoW movement (Realized + Baseline + Pipe) with Carlton / Judson before any external reference.

voiceover.text: Five asks. The top three are urgent: lock Kaiser before their Q4 renewal, convert HCA's thirty-two million in uncommitted pipe, and accelerate the Elevance Dagabah modernization. Operationally — double Factory attach from thirteen to twenty-five percent. And validate the one-point-three billion in week-over-week pipeline movement before referencing it externally.

data.quote: HLS's 23% YoY growth is masking a concentration problem: growth is flowing to Payors while Provider's 25-account long tail sits dormant.

voiceover.text: Bottom line — the growth headline is strong, but it's masking a structural concentration problem that needs fixing before Q4 renewals hit.

data.tagline: Diversify committed pipe. Protect Q4 renewals. Deepen Factory attach.

voiceover.text: Diversify committed pipe. Protect Q4 renewals. Deepen Factory attach. That's the playbook.
